name: Gorgon
description: Gorgons were monstrous feminine creatures in Greek mythology. While accounts of the number of Gorgons vary, the best-known are Stheno, Euryale, and Medusa, all daughters of the ancient sea deities Phorcys and Ceto. Medusa, unlike her sisters, was mortal. Gorgons were often depicted as having snakes for hair, sharp claws, and the power to turn anyone who looked directly at them into stone. Their terrifying appearance and deadly gaze made them formidable foes. Gorgons resided in remote and desolate locations, far from the reach of mortals. Their image was frequently used in apotropaic symbols, such as on shields and buildings, to ward off evil. The most famous Gorgon is Medusa, whose slaying by Perseus is one of the most well-known stories in Greek mythology. Perseus used a mirrored shield to avoid looking directly at Medusa and beheading her, later using her head as a weapon. The Gorgons embody primal fear and the dangerous power of the feminine in ancient Greek culture. Their legacy continues to resonate in art, literature, and popular culture.
subtitle: Petrifying gaze, serpentine hair.
appearance: Gorgons are typically depicted as monstrous women with snakes for hair, bronze hands, sharp teeth, and sometimes wings. Their faces are grotesque and terrifying, designed to instill fear in those who behold them. Medusa, before her transformation, was said to be a beautiful maiden. The snakes on their heads are often described as venomous and writhing, further adding to their horrifying appearance.
habitat: Remote and desolate locations, such as caves, islands, and the edges of the known world. They are often associated with dangerous and untamed environments.
behavior: Gorgons are generally portrayed as hostile and dangerous creatures, fiercely protective of their territory. They are driven by a primal rage and are quick to attack anyone who intrudes upon their domain. Medusa, in some accounts, is portrayed as a victim of circumstance, cursed by Athena for being defiled in her temple.

keyMyths.title: The Slaying of Medusa
keyMyths.description: Perseus, aided by Athena and Hermes, embarked on a quest to slay Medusa. Using a mirrored shield provided by Athena, he avoided her petrifying gaze and beheaded her, later using her head as a weapon against his enemies.

keyMyths.title: Medusa's Origin
keyMyths.description: Some accounts state that Medusa was originally a beautiful priestess of Athena, who was cursed by the goddess after being raped by Poseidon in her temple. This transformation into a Gorgon was a punishment for her defilement.

sources.author: Apollodorus
